pattern: Precomputing each index's monotone-run boundaries plus running min/max of prefix sums lets you, for every peak-valley pair, pick the best start and end in O(1) and take the maximum trionic sum.
hints: A trionic subarray is fixed by its peak (top of the first rise) and valley (bottom of the dip); the left start lies in the increasing run ending at the peak and the right end lies in the increasing run starting at the valley.
Expressing the subarray sum with prefix sums, for a fixed peak you want the smallest prefix at an allowed start, and for a fixed valley the largest prefix at an allowed end.

Compute, per index, the increasing run ending there (with a running minimum prefix) and the increasing run starting there (with a running maximum prefix), and the decreasing run after each peak; then sweep peaks and the valleys reachable down from them, combining the two precomputed extremes.

edge_inputs.description: longer runs let extending past the valley help or hurt
two candidate windows, the larger-sum one wins
all-negative values force a negative best
trailing rise should be extended to its peak when positive

stress.description: short random arrays over a narrow range so many overlapping trionic windows form
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(4, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(-9, 9) for _ in range(n)]
    # guarantee at least one trionic window exists
    nums[0:4] = [0, 5, 2, 7]
    return [nums]
stress.note: size bounded so the cubic oracle stays fast; seeded prefix guarantees a trionic window
